completionPraises: That was so good my screen is doing a happy dance 💃📱
High five in the air, I can't reach you 🙌
Sparkles incoming... 🌟🌟🌟 because you EARNED it
You're cooking now! 🍳🔥 (with words, not eggs)
That deserves a silly dance break 🕺

sections.title: Short Vowels
sections.tip: Short vowels make a quick, soft sound!
sections.rule: Short vowels appear in closed syllables — a vowel followed by a consonant.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say 'ahh' like at the doctor. Your mouth opens wide.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: One vowel A between two consonants — it almost always makes this short sound.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Think of the Hindi short 'अ' sound — very similar! 'Cat' sounds like 'kae-t', not 'kay-t'.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't stretch the A. 'Cat' is short and crisp, not 'caay-t'.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The fat cat sat on a mat.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say 'eh' — like when you are confused. Short and flat.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Closed syllable with E between consonants. The mouth is slightly open, lips spread.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Similar to the 'ए' sound in Hindi but much shorter and flatter — don't let it become 'ay'.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: 'Bed' is not 'bayd'. Keep it flat and quick.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The red hen went to bed at ten.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say 'ih' — very short, like a quick tick. Lips slightly spread.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: I in a closed syllable. Very quick sound — don't hold it.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Like the short 'इ' in Hindi. 'Sit' sounds like 'siht' not 'seet'.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Indian speakers often make this too long — 'fish' should NOT sound like 'feesh'.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The big fish can sit and win.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say 'oh' but very short — mouth open and round, jaw drops.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: O in a closed syllable. American English: sounds like 'ah' (dog = 'dahg').
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: This one is tricky! American short O sounds like 'ah' — 'hot' sounds like 'haht', not like Hindi 'hote'.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't make it a full 'oh' sound. 'Dog' is 'dahg', 'top' is 'tahp'.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The hot dog sat on top of the box.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say 'uh' — like a relaxed grunt. Mouth barely open, very neutral.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: U in a closed syllable. Very relaxed sound — the most neutral vowel in English.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Similar to the schwa sound in English. 'Cup' is 'kuhp', not 'koop' or 'kap'.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't make it 'oo'. 'Sun' is 'suhn', not 'soon'. 'Duck' is 'duhk', not 'dook'.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The sun is up — run, duck, run!

sections.title: Long Vowels
sections.tip: Long vowels say their name — like when you say the alphabet!
sections.rule: Magic E Rule: vowel + consonant + e = long vowel!

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say the letter name A — 'ay'. Mouth opens then closes slightly.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Magic E: vowel + consonant + silent e = long vowel. Also: ai, ay vowel teams.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: This is like the Hindi 'ए' held a bit longer. 'Cake' = 'kayk', nice and clear.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't confuse with short A. 'Cap' (short) vs 'cape' (long) — the E changes everything.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: Can you bake a cake on a rainy day?

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say the letter name E — 'ee'. Lips spread wide like a smile.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Long E appears with ee, ea, or at the end of short words (me, he, she, be).
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Like 'ई' in Hindi. 'Tree' = 'tree', clear and long. Easy for Indian speakers!
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Watch out for 'ea' — it doesn't always say long E. 'Bread' says short E!
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: She can see the green tree by the sea.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say the letter name I — 'eye'. Mouth opens wide then closes.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Magic E pattern is very common here: bike, time, kite, hide. Also: -ight words.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Like 'आई' gliding together quickly. 'Bike' = 'bayyk'. Glide from 'ah' to 'ee'.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't say 'bick' for bike or 'tim' for time. The E is silent but powerful!
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: I like to ride my bike and fly my kite.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say the letter name O — 'oh'. Lips round and push forward slightly.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Magic E: home, rope, note. Also: oa, ow vowel teams (boat, snow).
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Like Hindi 'ओ' but lips round more. 'Home' = 'hohm', clean and round.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't flatten it. 'Home' is not 'ham'. Round your lips!
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: I rode home on the road past the stone.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say the letter name U — 'yoo'. Lips round and push forward.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Magic E: cute, tube, cube. Open syllable: music (mu-sic, the mu is open so U says its name).
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Like 'यू' in Hindi. 'Cute' = 'kyoot'. Note: 'music' is long U because the syllable 'mu' is open — no consonant closes it.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't say 'coot' for cute — it needs the 'y' glide: 'kyoot'.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The cute mule played music on the tube.

sections.title: Vowel Teams
sections.tip: Two vowels work together to make one sound!

teacherNotes.howToSay: Long A sound — 'ay'. Same as Magic E long A but made by two vowels together.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: AI is used in the middle of a word (rain, tail). AY is used at the end (day, play). Remember: AI in the middle, AY at the end.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Easy rule: if the 'ay' sound is at the end of the word, write AY. If it's in the middle, write AI.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't write 'dayn' — it should be 'rain' (AI in middle) or 'day' (AY at end).
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The train came on a rainy day.

items.label: ee / ea — Long E
items.note: ea has 3 sounds: long E (eat, leaf), short E (bread, head), long A (pear, bear)

teacherNotes.howToSay: Usually long E — 'ee'. But EA is tricky — it has THREE sounds!
teacherNotes.simpleRule: EE always says long E (feet, tree). EA usually says long E (leaf, eat) but watch out for exceptions.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: EE is safe and predictable. EA needs memorizing per word because it changes.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: EA exceptions to know: bread/head/dead = short E. Break/steak/pear = long A. Most EA = long E.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: Eat your meat but not the bread — it's a treat!

items.label: oa / ow — Long O
items.note: ow can also make the ouch sound as in cow, town, down

teacherNotes.howToSay: Long O sound — 'oh'. OA in the middle, OW often at the end.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: OA in the middle of a word (boat, road, soap). OW at the end (snow, blow, grow). But careful — OW can also say 'ow!' as in cow, town.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: OW has two jobs! At the end of a word it usually says 'oh' (snow, flow). But before N it often says 'ow!' (town, down, brown).
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Know vs now — same OW, different sounds! Context and spelling position help.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The boat floats on the road after the snow melts.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Two sounds! Long OO = 'oo' as in moon (lips round, pushed forward). Short OO = 'uh' as in book (shorter, lips less rounded).
teacherNotes.simpleRule: No reliable rule for which OO sound to use — these must be memorized per word. Moon/food/spoon = long OO. Book/look/wood = short OO.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Long OO is like Hindi 'ऊ'. Short OO is halfway between 'oo' and 'u'. When in doubt, try both and see which sounds like a real word!
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't say 'bool' for 'bull' or 'fude' for 'food'. These need memorizing.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: Look at the moon with a spoon of food!

items.label: ui / ue — Long U
items.note: Sometimes ui makes a long I sound as in guide, build

teacherNotes.howToSay: Usually long U — 'yoo' or 'oo'. UE at the end (blue, glue). UI in the middle (fruit, suit).
teacherNotes.simpleRule: UE at end of word = long U (blue, true, clue). UI in middle = usually long U (fruit, juice) but sometimes long I (guide, build).
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Blue, glue, true — these are easy. For UI words, just memorize which sound — fruit='oo', guide='eye'.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Build and guide have UI but say long I, not long U. These are exceptions to memorize.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: Drink blue juice from the fruit — it's true!

teacherNotes.howToSay: The 'oi' diphthong — glides from 'oh' to 'ee'. Unique sound, not in most Indian languages.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: OI in the middle of a word (coin, boil, join). OY at the end (boy, toy, joy). Same rule as AI/AY!
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: This sound doesn't exist in Hindi or Telugu — needs practice. Say 'oh' then quickly say 'ee': 'oh-ee' = 'oi'. 'Boy' = 'boh-ee' said fast.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't say 'ball' for 'boil' or 'bay' for 'boy'. The glide from oh to ee is the key.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The boy found a coin — what a joy!

teacherNotes.howToSay: The 'ow' diphthong — glides from 'ah' to 'oo'. Like when you say 'ouch!'
teacherNotes.simpleRule: OU in the middle (house, cloud, loud). OW at the end or before N (cow, town, brown). Remember OW has two sounds — this is the 'ouch' one.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Say 'aa' then quickly say 'oo': 'aa-oo' = 'ow'. 'House' = 'haa-oos' said fast. Practice with: ow! ouch! wow!
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't confuse with long O OW (snow=oh) vs this OW (cow=ow). Position in word helps: before N usually 'ow!' sound.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The loud cow ran out of the house in town.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Long A sound — 'ay'. EIGH is a group of 4 letters making one sound!
teacherNotes.simpleRule: EI and EIGH both make long A. EIGH words: eight, weight, sleigh, freight. EI words: vein, rein, their.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: The GH in EIGH is completely silent — eight is just 'ayt', not 'ayght'. These are high-frequency words worth memorizing.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't pronounce the GH. Eight = 'ayt'. Weight = 'wayt'. The whole -EIGH is just the 'ay' sound.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: Eight friends came to weigh the sleigh.

sections.title: Digraphs
sections.tip: Two consonants team up to make ONE brand new sound!
sections.rule: Sh, ch, th, ph, and wh each make a single new sound — say it once, not letter by letter!

teacherNotes.howToSay: Round your lips like you're saying 'shh, be quiet!' and push air out softly — no buzzing sound.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Sh makes one quiet hissing sound — it can start a word (shop) or end one (fish).
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Sh matches श in Hindi/Telugu almost exactly — this one should feel natural with no traps.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't say 's' and 'h' separately — it's one smooth, quiet sound.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: She had a fish and a shiny shell.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Touch your tongue just behind your top teeth, then release with a little puff of air — like the start of a sneeze.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Ch makes one sound, whether it starts a word (chip) or ends one (lunch).
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Ch matches च in Hindi/Telugu closely — another one that should transfer easily.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Watch out for tricky words like 'school' (ch = /k/) and 'chef' (ch = /sh/) — not every ch says /ch/!
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The chubby chick munched on a peach.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Stick your tongue out just a little, resting it between your top and bottom teeth, then blow air gently.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Th can sound soft like in 'thin' or buzzy like in 'this' — your tongue peeks out either way.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: This is the tricky one — Hindi/Telugu don't have this sound, so we often swap it for 't' or 'd' ('think' becomes 'tink'). Slow down and practice the tongue-between-teeth position.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't replace th with a t or d sound — let the tongue peek out so air can pass through.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The thin thief thought about three things.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Ph looks like two letters but it just says /f/ — gently bite your bottom lip and blow.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Ph always sounds like f — it's an old Greek spelling, found in words like 'phone' and 'elephant.'
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Careful — in Hindi फ is a hard, puffed 'p' sound. English ph is nothing like that; it's a soft 'f', same as in 'fish.'
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't say a 'p' sound for ph — say 'f' instead ('phone' sounds like 'fone,' not 'pone').
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The elephant used the phone to call her nephew.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Push a little puff of air out as you say it — 'hw' — lips rounded like you're about to whistle.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: W and H together make one sound at the start of a word — say it like you're blowing out a candle.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Hindi/Telugu speakers often flatten 'wh' to a plain 'w' (whale sounds like 'wail') — the puff of air gets dropped. Exaggerate the breath on 'wh' words until it feels silly-obvious.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Mixing up 'wh' words with plain 'w' words that sound almost identical (whale/wail, white/wight) — lean on spelling, not just sound.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The whale blew a whistle-shaped spray of white water.

sections.title: Blends
sections.tip: Blends let you hear BOTH consonant sounds — just said quickly together!
sections.rule: Unlike digraphs, blends keep each letter's own sound — say l-blends and r-blends fast, but don't skip either letter!

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say 'p' and then quickly slide into 'l' — puh-luh blended fast into one beat.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Pl keeps both sounds — you can hear the p AND the l, just said quickly together.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: We sometimes slip a tiny extra vowel sound in between ('puh-lane' instead of 'plane') — practice saying both letters with no gap.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't drop either sound — both the p and the l need to be heard.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: Please plant the plum tree by the playground.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say 'b' and roll right into 'r' — buh-ruh, fast and smooth.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Br keeps both the b sound and the r sound blended together.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: English 'r' is softer and less rolled than Hindi/Telugu र — try a light, quick r instead of a strong roll.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't let br turn into just 'b' — make sure the r sound shows up too.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The brave bride crossed the bridge with a bright brush.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say 'c/k' and quickly add 'l' — kuh-luh blended into one sound.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Cl keeps both the k sound and the l sound together.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: This one usually transfers well — just don't add an extra vowel sound between the two letters.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't say it as two separate beats ('cuh-luh') — blend it into one quick sound.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The clever clown clapped and climbed the clock tower.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say 'c/k' and quickly add 'r' — kuh-ruh blended fast.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Cr keeps both the k sound and the r sound together.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Keep the English 'r' light and quick — not a heavy rolled र.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't skip the r — 'crab' shouldn't sound like 'cab.'
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The crab crawled across the crunchy, cracked ice.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say 'f' and quickly add 'l' — fuh-luh blended into one smooth sound.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Fl keeps both the f sound and the l sound together.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Should transfer easily — just don't add an extra vowel sound between the f and the l.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't drop the l — 'flag' shouldn't sound like 'fag.'
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The flying flag flapped above the flower field.

sections.title: C, K, and CK Spelling Rules
sections.tip: Same /k/ sound — but which spelling do you use?

items.label: CK — after a short vowel
items.rule: Use CK right after a short vowel at the end of a word or syllable

teacherNotes.howToSay: The /k/ sound — same as C and K. CK is just a spelling choice, not a new sound.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Use CK immediately after a SHORT vowel at the end of a word or syllable. The short vowel + CK is a package: ack, eck, ick, ock, uck.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Quick test: is there a short vowel RIGHT before the /k/ sound with nothing in between? Yes = CK. Duck = short U + CK. Sock = short O + CK.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't use CK at the beginning of words (never ckite, ckap). And not after consonants — park uses K not CK because R comes before K.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The duck wore a black sock on a truck.

items.label: K — after consonant, vowel team, or long vowel
items.rule: Use K after a consonant, two vowels, or a long vowel sound

teacherNotes.howToSay: Same /k/ sound. K is used when CK would be wrong.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Use K after: a consonant (park, milk, silk), a vowel team/long vowel (book, week, bike, take), or before E/I/Y at the start of a word (keep, kind, sky).
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: K before E and I at the start: kind, keep, kitchen, king. This is because C before E/I makes a soft sound (like S) — so we use K to keep the /k/ sound.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Don't write 'ceep' for keep or 'cind' for kind — C before E/I says /s/, so use K!
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: Keep the book in the dark park until the week ends.

items.label: C — before a, o, u or start of most words
items.rule: Use C before a, o, u and in most multi-syllable words. Use K before e, i, y.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Same /k/ sound — but C also makes an /s/ sound before E, I, Y.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: C says /k/ before A, O, U (cat, coat, cup) and at start of most words. C says /s/ before E, I, Y (city, cycle, cent). In multi-syllable words ending in -ic, always use C (picnic, comic, music).
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: C is the default /k/ letter. Only switch to K when the next letter is E, I, or Y. So: cat=C, kind=K, cup=C, keep=K.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Music ends in C not K — because multi-syllable words use C at the end (-ic pattern). Picnic, comic, magic all follow this rule.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The cat at the picnic drank from a cup of cocoa.

sections.title: Hard & Soft G
sections.tip: G says two sounds — hard like 'go' or soft like 'giraffe'!
sections.rule: G is usually soft (/j/) before e, i, y — hard (/g/) everywhere else.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say /g/ like in 'go' — sound comes from the back of your throat.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Hard G sounds like the letter name G. Usually before a, o, u, or another consonant.
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: Same as Hindi ग (ga) — no difference in mouth shape, this one's easy.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: None typical — hard G maps cleanly to native sounds.
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The goat ate the gum.

teacherNotes.howToSay: Say /j/ like in 'jump' — G borrows the J sound here.
teacherNotes.simpleRule: Soft G sounds like J. Happens before e, i, or y (gem, giant, gym).
teacherNotes.indianDadTip: This is the tricky one — G looking like J has no parallel in Hindi/Telugu, where ग is always hard. Point out it 'sounds like J' explicitly.
teacherNotes.commonMistake: Reading soft-G words with the hard /g/ sound (saying 'giraffe' with a hard g like 'go').
teacherNotes.exampleSentence: The giant giraffe ate a gem.
